name: Magnesium
effects: Supports normal muscle and nerve function
contraindications: Use caution with renal impairment due to higher hypermagnesemia risk
High supplemental doses can cause gastrointestinal adverse effects

sources.title: Magnesium Fact Sheet for Health Professionals (NIH ODS)

description: Magnesium (Mg2+) is supplied by foods and supplements (for example citrate, oxide, chloride, and aspartate forms). About half of body magnesium is in bone, with the remainder in muscle and other soft tissues.
summary: Magnesium is an essential mineral involved in 300+ enzymatic reactions related to energy metabolism and muscle/nerve function; supplementation is primarily framed around adequacy and intake targets.
mechanism: Established with proposed extensions: magnesium is a cofactor in ATP-linked enzymatic reactions and helps regulate calcium/potassium transport affecting neuromuscular excitability; migraine-related benefits remain proposed/context dependent.
safetyNotes: Generally safe at dietary intake levels.
Higher supplemental doses can cause diarrhea and other gastrointestinal effects (often formulation dependent).
Overdose can cause hypermagnesemia, with higher risk in renal impairment.
interactions: Can reduce absorption of bisphosphonates and some oral antibiotics (including tetracyclines and fluoroquinolones); dose separation is commonly advised.
May potentiate neuromuscular blockers in medical contexts.
dosage: RDA framework (adults): about 310–420 mg/day from total intake.
Supplement products commonly provide about 100–400 mg elemental magnesium/day.
Medical IV dosing (for example magnesium sulfate in eclampsia) is separate from routine supplement use.
preparation: Common supplemental forms include oxide, citrate, chloride, and aspartate salts; food sources include nuts, leafy greens, and whole grains.
regulatoryStatus: Essential nutrient with established dietary reference intakes; sold as a dietary supplement and used in prescription products in specific medical settings.
